plainMeaning: May my homage — for past, present, and future — go out to the four ever-existing Jinas: Rishabhanana, Chandranana, Vardhamana, and Varishena.
recitation: Ṛṣabhānana, Candrānana, Vardhamāna, Vāriṣeṇa, e cāra śāśvatā jinane mārī trikāla vandanā hojo.

wordByWord.gloss: Rishabhanana, the first of the four eternal Jinas
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ṛṣabha ('bull'; also the name of the first Tīrthaṅkara) + ānana ('face, countenance') — 'the Ṛṣabha-faced / bull-faced one'.
wordByWord.notes: A proper name of a śāśvata-jina, not the historical Ṛṣabhanātha of our cauvīsī, though the names echo each other.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. candra ('moon') + ānana ('face') — 'the moon-faced one', whose countenance is serene and luminous as the moon.
Skt. present participle of √vṛdh ('to grow, increase') — 'the ever-increasing / flourishing one'. The same name borne by Mahāvīra, but here a distinct eternal Jina.
wordByWord.notes: Do not conflate with Mahāvīra-Vardhamāna of the present era; sharing a name does not mean sharing identity.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vāri ('water') + seṇa (Skt. sena, 'army, host') — traditionally read 'lord of a host as boundless as the waters'.
wordByWord.notes: Spelled Vāriṣeṇa / Vārisena in different editions.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati demonstrative, from Skt. ete / etad ('these, this').
wordByWord.notes: Gathers the four named Jinas into a single object of homage.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati numeral, from Skt. catur, via Pkt. cattāri ('four').
Skt. śāśvata ('perpetual, everlasting'), from śaśvat ('perpetually, again and again'); here in plural agreement.
jina, from √ji ('to conquer') — 'victor over the inner passions' + Gujarati dative/accusative postposition -ne ('to').
wordByWord.notes: The -ne marks the four Jinas as recipients of the vandanā.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati first-person possessive, from Skt. madīya / mama ('mine'); feminine singular, agreeing with vandanā.

wordByWord.gloss: for the three times — past, present, and future
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tri ('three') + kāla ('time'). See preface glossary 'trikāla-vandanā'.
wordByWord.notes: Adverbial-adjectival, qualifying the homage as offered across all time.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vandanā, from √vand ('to praise, salute, revere').

wordByWord.gloss: may it be / let it be
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati precative-optative of the verb 'to be', from √bhū (ho-) — 'may (it) be'.
wordByWord.notes: The benedictive mood turns the line from statement into aspiration: 'may my homage be (offered).'

literalTranslation: Rishabhanana, Chandranana, Vardhamana, Varishena — to these four eternal Jinas may my threefold (past-present-future) homage be.
idiomaticTranslation: Rishabhanana, Chandranana, Vardhamana, Varishena — to these four eternal Jinas, undying through every turn of time, may my reverence go out across past, present, and future alike.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the preceding line bowed to the three cauvīsīs — the twenty-four-fold sets of past, future, and present — this verse turns to a different register of the eternal. The cauvīsīs are time-bound assemblies that arise and pass with each half-cycle; the four named here are the śāśvata-jinas, whose names and images never change across any kalpa. Naming exactly four, and no more, is doctrinally precise: tradition holds these four as the fixed set installed in the eternal shrines (śāśvata-caitya) of the cosmos. The poet sets the proper names first, bare and unadorned, then collects them with 'e cāra śāśvatā jinane' — 'to these four eternal Jinas' — so the salutation lands on all four at once. Note that two of the names, Ṛṣabha(-ānana) and Vardhamāna, deliberately echo the first and last Tīrthaṅkaras of our era; the verse trades on that resonance while keeping the referents distinct — an eternal archetype standing behind the historical figure. The closing 'mārī trikāla vandanā hojo' is the structural refrain of this whole salutation movement, but here it gains a special fitness: a threefold (past-present-future) homage is exactly the homage suited to Jinas who themselves abide through all three times. Time-spanning reverence meets time-spanning objects of reverence.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain doctrine of śāśvata (eternal) Jina-images: in Jain cosmology certain caityas and their bimbas are anādi-ananta — without beginning or end — and the four Jinas Ṛṣabhānana, Candrānana, Vardhamāna, and Vāriṣeṇa are their perpetual occupants, recurring identically in every time-cycle. This expresses two principles at once: that the dharma itself is beginningless (the path to mokṣa is always available), and that veneration is owed not only to the Jinas of our measurable history but to the timeless ideal they instantiate. Bowing 'trikāla' to beings who are themselves śāśvata aligns the reciter's act of devotion with the eternity of its object.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Concluding the sāmāyika, the reciter widens devotion beyond the familiar twenty-four to the eternal Jinas, training the mind to revere the path as something timeless rather than tied to one era or one shrine. Practically, one pauses on each of the four names, then releases the whole aspiration with 'hojo' — letting the homage be an offering, not a transaction.

crossReferences.text: Concept of śāśvata-caitya and the four eternal Jina-images recurring in every kalpa
Preceding verse — trikāla homage to the atīta / anāgata / vartamāna cauvīsīs
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.), samayik-parvani v.5

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of Jina/Tīrthaṅkara, the avasarpiṇī/utsarpiṇī time-cycles, and the distinction between era-bound and eternal Jinas.

sources.title: Shashvata (definition)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of śāśvata ('eternal, perpetual') and its application to śāśvata-jina / śāśvata-caitya in Jain cosmology.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Roots and glosses for ānana, candra, vṛdh/vardhamāna, vāri, √vand (vandanā), √ji (jina), kāla.

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipakṣa edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse wording and its placement within the trikāla-vandanā salutation block of the sāmāyika conclusion.

notes: The four śāśvata-jina names are standardly transmitted but their listed order and the exact spelling of Vāriṣeṇa (Vāriṣeṇa / Vārisena) vary across editions; this rendering follows the verse's own order (Ṛṣabhānana, Candrānana, Vardhamāna, Vāriṣeṇa), which differs from some Digambara listings that pair Vāriṣeṇa before Vardhamāna. The verse is in Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa devotional idiom; postpositions -ne and the precative hojo are Gujarati, while the four names are Sanskrit. The śāśvata-jina images here are eternal archetypes and should not be identified with the same-named historical Tīrthaṅkaras Ṛṣabhanātha and Mahāvīra-Vardhamāna of our cauvīsī. Original orthography preserved per the book's fidelity rules.
